volumeTitle: The Shield. Catalogue of Women. Other Fragments
label: The Shield

sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: ἢ οἵη προλιποῦσα δόμους καὶ πατρίδα γαῖαν
sections.translation: Or like her who left home and country and came to Thebes , following warlike Amphitryon ,— even Alcmena , the daughter of Electryon , gatherer of the people . She surpassed the tribe of womankind

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: εἴδεΐ τε μεγέθει τε · νόον γε μὲν οὔ τις ἔριζε
sections.translation: in beauty and in height ; and in wisdom none vied with her of those whom mortal women bore of union with mortal men . Her face and her dark eyes wafted such charm as comes from golden Aphrodite . And she so honored her husband in her heart

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ὡς οὔ πώ τις ἔτισε γυναικῶν θηλυτεράων ·
sections.translation: as none of womankind did before her . In truth he had slain her noble father violently when he was angry about oxen ; so he left his own country and came to Thebes and was suppliant to the shield-carrying men of Cadmus . There he dwelt with his modest wife

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: νόσφιν ἄτερ φιλότητος ἐφιμέρου , οὐδέ οἱ ἦεν
sections.translation: without the joys of love , nor might he go in unto the neat-ankled daughter of Electryon until he had avenged the death of his wife ' s great-hearted brothers and utterly burned with blazing fire the villages of the heroes , the Taphians and Teleboans ;

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: τὼς γάρ οἱ διέκειτο , θεοὶ δʼ ἐπὶ μάρτυροι ἦσαν ·
sections.translation: for this thing was laid upon him , and the gods were witnesses to it . And he feared their anger , and hastened to perform the great task to which Zeus had bound him . With him went the horse-driving Boeotians , breathing above their shields ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: Λοκροί τʼ ἀγχέμαχοι καὶ Φωκῆες μεγάθυμοι
sections.translation: and the Locrians who fight hand to hand , and the gallant Phocians eager for war and battle . And the noble son of Alcaeus led them , rejoicing in his host . But the father of men and gods was forming another scheme in his heart , to beget one to defend against destruction gods and men who eat bread .

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: ὦρτο δʼ ἀπʼ Οὐλύμποιο δόλον φρεσὶ βυσσοδομεύων ,
sections.translation: So he arose from Olympus by night pondering guile in the deep of his heart , and yearned for the love of the well-girded woman . Quickly he came to Typhaonium , and from there again wise Zeus went on and trod the highest peak of Phicium : [*] there he sat and planned marvellous things in his heart .

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: αὐτῇ μὲν γὰρ νυκτὶ τανυσφύρου Ἠλεκτρυώνης
sections.translation: So in one night Zeus shared the bed and love of the neat-ankled daughter of Electryon and fulfilled his desire ; and in the same night Amphitryon , gatherer of the people , the glorious hero , came to his house when he had ended his great task . 39 He hastened not to go to his bondmen and shepherds afield ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: ὦρτʼ ἰέναι , πρίν γʼ ἧς ἀλόχου ἐπιβήμεναι εὐνῆς ·
sections.translation: but first went in to his wife : such desire took hold on the shepherd of the people . And as a man who has escaped joyfully from misery , whether of sore disease or cruel bondage , so then did Amphitryon , when he had wound up all his heavy task ,

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: ἀσπασίως τε φίλως τε ἑὸν δόμον εἰσαφίκανεν .
sections.translation: come glad and welcome to his home . And all night long he lay with his modest wife , delighting in the gifts of golden Aphrodite . And she , being subject in love to a god and to a man exceeding goodly , brought forth twin sons in seven-gated Thebes .

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: οὐ καθʼ ὁμὰ φρονέοντε · κασιγνήτω γε μὲν ἤστην ·
sections.translation: Though they were brothers , these were not of one spirit ; for one was weaker but the other a far better man , one terrible and strong , the mighty Heracles . She bore him through the embrace of the son of Cronos lord of dark clouds and the other , Iphicles , of Amphitryon the spear-wielder —

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: κεκριμένην γενεήν · τὸν μὲν βροτῷ ἀνδρὶ μιγεῖσα ,
sections.translation: offspring distinct , this one of union with a mortal man , but that other of union with Zeus , leader of all the gods . And he slew Cycnus , the gallant son of Ares . For he found him in the precinct of far-shooting Apollo , him and his father Ares , never sated with war .

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: τεύχεσι λαμπομένους σέλας ὣς πυρὸς αἰθομένοιο ,
sections.translation: Their armour shone like a flame of blazing fire as they two stood in their chariot : their swift horses struck the earth and pawed it with their hoofs , and the dust rose like smoke about them , pounded by the chariot wheels and the horses ' hoofs , while the well-made chariot and its rails rattled around them

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: ἵππων ἱεμένων · κεχάρητο δὲ Κύκνος ἀμύμων ,
sections.translation: as the horses plunged . And blameless Cycnus was glad , for he hoped to slay the warlike son of Zeus and his charioteer with the sword , and to strip off their splendid armour . But Phoebus Apollo would not listen to his vaunts , for he himself had stirred up mighty Heracles against him .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: πᾶν δʼ ἄλσος καὶ βωμὸς Ἀπόλλωνος Παγασαίου
sections.translation: And all the grove and altar of Pagasaean Apollo flamed because of the dread god and because of his arms ; for his eyes flashed as with fire . What mortal man would have dared to meet him face to face save Heracles and glorious Iolaus ?

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: κείνων γὰρ μεγάλη τε βίη καὶ χεῖρες ἄαπτοι
sections.translation: For great was their strength and unconquerable were the arms which grew from their shoulders on their strong limbs . Then Heracles spake to his charioteer strong Iolaus : 78 “ O hero Iolaus , best beloved of all men , truly Amphitryon sinned deeply against the blessed gods who dwell on Olympus

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: ὣς φάτο · μείδησεν δὲ βίη Ἡρακληείη
sections.translation: So he said . And mighty Heracles was glad in heart and smiled , for the other ' s words pleased him well , and he answered him with winged words : “ O hero Iolaus , heaven-sprung , now is rough battle hard at hand .

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: ὣς καὶ νῦν μέγαν ἵππον Ἀρίονα κυανοχαίτην
sections.translation: But , as you have shown your skill at other times , so now also wheel the great black-maned horse Arion about every way , and help me as you may be able .” So he said , and put upon his legs greaves of shining bronze , the splendid gift of Hephaestus . Next he fastened about his breast

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: καλὸν χρύσειον πολυδαίδαλον , ὅν οἱ ἔδωκε
sections.translation: a fine golden breast-plate , curiously wrought , which Pallas Athena the daughter of Zeus had given him when first he was about to set out upon his grievous labors . Over his shoulders the fierce warrior put the steel that saves men from doom , and across his breast

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: καββάλετʼ ἐξόπιθεν · πολλοὶ δʼ ἔντοσθεν ὀιστοὶ
sections.translation: he slung behind him a hollow quiver . Within it were many chilling arrows , dealers of death which makes speech forgotten : in front they had death , and trickled with tears ; their shafts were smooth and very long ; and their butts were covered with feathers of a brown eagle .

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: εἵλετο δʼ ὄβριμον ἔγχος , ἀκαχμένον αἴθοπι χαλκῷ ,
sections.translation: And he took his strong spear , pointed with shining bronze , and on his valiant head set a well-made helm of adamant , cunningly wrought , which fitted closely on the temples ; and that guarded the head of god-like Heracles . In his hands he took his shield , all glittering : no one ever

sections.label: Section 140
sections.original: οὔτʼ ἔρρηξε βαλὼν οὔτʼ ἔθλασε , θαῦμα ἰδέσθαι .
sections.translation: broke it with a blow or crushed it . And a wonder it was to see ; for its whole orb shimmered with enamel and white ivory and electrum , and it glowed with shining gold ; and there were zones of cyanus [*] drawn upon it . In the center was Fear worked in adamant , unspeakable ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: ἔμπαλιν ὄσσοισιν πυρὶ λαμπομένοισι δεδορκώς ·
sections.translation: staring backwards with eyes that glowed with fire . His mouth was full of teeth in a white row , fearful and daunting , and upon his grim brow hovered frightful Strife who arrays the throng of men : pitiless she , for she took away the mind and senses of poor wretches

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: οἵτινες ἀντιβίην πόλεμον Διὸς υἷι φέροιεν .
sections.translation: who made war against the son of Zeus . Their souls passed beneath the earth and went down into the house of Hades ; but their bones , when the skin is rotted about them , crumble away on the dark earth under parching Sirius . 154 Upon the shield Pursuit and Flight were wrought ,

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: ἐν δʼ Ὅμαδός τε Φόβος τʼ Ἀνδροκτασίη τε δεδήει ,
sections.translation: and Tumult , and Panic , and Slaughter . Strife also , and Uproar were hurrying about , and deadly Fate was there holding one man newly wounded , and another unwounded ; and one , who was dead , she was dragging by the feet through the tumult . She had on her shoulders a garment red with the blood of men ,

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: δεινὸν δερκομένη καναχῇσί τε βεβρυχυῖα .
sections.translation: and terribly she glared and gnashed her teeth . And there were heads of snakes unspeakably frightful , twelve of them ; and they used to frighten the tribes of men on earth made war against the son of Zeus ; for they would clash their teeth when Amphitryon ' s son was fighting :

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: Ἀμφιτρυωνιάδης , τὰ δʼ ἐδαίετο θαυματὰ ἔργα .
sections.translation: and brightly shone these wonderful works . And it was as though there were spots upon the frightful snakes : and their backs were dark blue and their jaws were black . Also there were upon the shield droves of boars and lions who glared at each other , being furious and eager :

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: τῶν καὶ ὁμιληδὸν στίχες ἤισαν · οὐδέ νυ τώ γε
sections.translation: the rows of them moved on together , and neither side trembled but both bristled up their manes . For already a great lion lay between them and two boars , one on either side , bereft of life , and their dark blood was dripping down upon the ground ;

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: κείατο τεθνηῶτες ὑπὸ βλοσυροῖσι λέουσιν .
sections.translation: they lay dead with necks outstretched beneath the grim lions . And both sides were roused still more to fight because they were angry , the fierce boars and the bright-eyed lions . 178 And there was the strife of the Lapith spearmen gathered round the prince Caeneus and Dryas and Peirithous ,

sections.label: Section 180
sections.original: Ὁπλέα τʼ Ἐξάδιόν τε Φάληρόν τε Πρόλοχόν τε
sections.translation: with Hopleus , Exadius , Phalereus , and Prolochus , Mopsus the son of Ampyce of Titaresia , a scion of Ares , and Theseus , the son of Aegeus , like the deathless gods . These were of silver , and had armor of gold upon their bodies . And the Centaurs were gathered against them on the other side

sections.label: Section 185
sections.original: ἀμφὶ μέγαν Πετραῖον ἰδʼ Ἄσβολον οἰωνιστὴν
sections.translation: with Petraeus and Asbolus the diviner , Arctus , and Ureus , and black-haired Mimas , and the two sons of Peuceus , Perimedes and Dryalus : these were of silver , and they had pinetrees of gold in their hands , and they were rushing together as though they were alive

sections.label: Section 190
sections.original: ἔγχεσιν ἠδʼ ἐλάτῃς αὐτοσχεδὸν ὠριγνῶντο .
sections.translation: and striking at one another hand to hand with spears and with pines . And on the shield stood the fleet-footed horses of grim Ares made of gold , and deadly Ares the spoil-winner himself . He held a spear in his hands and was urging on the footmen : he was red with blood as if he were slaying living men ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: δίφρου ἐπεμβεβαώς · παρὰ δὲ Δεῖμός τε Φόβος τε
sections.translation: and he stood in his chariot . Beside him stood Fear and Flight , eager to plunge amidst the fighting men . There , too , was the daughter of Zeus , Tritogeneia who drives the spoil . [*] She was like as if she would array a battle , with a spear in her hand , and a golden helmet ,

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: αἰγίδα τʼ ἀμφʼ ὤμοις · ἐπὶ δʼ ᾤχετο φύλοπιν αἰνήν .
sections.translation: and the aegis about her shoulders . And she was going towards the awful strife . And there was the holy company of the deathless gods : and in the midst the son of Zeus and Leto played sweetly on a golden lyre . There also was the abode of the gods , pure Olympus , and their assembly , and infinite riches were spread around

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: ἀθανάτων ἐν ἀγῶνι · θεαὶ δʼ ἐξῆρχον ἀοιδῆς
sections.translation: in the gathering of the deathless gods . Also the goddesses , the Muses of Pieria were beginning a song like clear-voiced singers . And on the shield was a harbor with a safe haven from the irresistible sea , made of refined tin wrought in a circle , and it seemed to heave with waves . In the middle of it were

sections.label: Section 210
sections.original: δελφῖνες τῇ καὶ τῇ ἐθύνεον ἰχθυάοντες
sections.translation: many dolphins rushing this way and that , fishing : and they seemed to be swimming . Two dolphins of silver were spouting and devouring the mute fishes . And beneath them fishes of bronze were trembling . And on the shore sat a fisherman watching : in his hands he held

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: ἰχθύσιν ἀμφίβληστρον ἀπορρίψοντι ἐοικώς .
sections.translation: a casting net for fish , and seemed as if about to cast it forth . 216 There , too , was the son of rich-haired Danae , the horseman Perseus : his feet did not touch the shield and yet were not far from it — very marvellous to remark , since he was not supported anywhere ; for so did the famous Lame One fashion him of gold with his hands .

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: χρύσεον · ἀμφὶ δὲ ποσσὶν ἔχεν πτερόεντα πέδιλα .
sections.translation: On his feet he had winged sandals , and his black-sheathed sword was slung across his shoulders by a cross-belt of bronze . He was flying swift as thought . The head of a dreadful monster , the Gorgon , covered the broad of his back , and a bag of silver — a marvel to see —

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: ἀργυρέη · θύσανοι δὲ κατῃωρεῦντο φαεινοὶ
sections.translation: contained it : and from the bag bright tassels of gold hung down . Upon the head of the hero lay the dread cap [*] of Hades which had the awful gloom of night . Perseus himself , the son of Danae , was at full stretch , like one who hurries and shudders with horror . And after him rushed

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: Γοργόνες ἄπλητοί τε καὶ οὐ φαταὶ ἐρρώοντο
sections.translation: the Gorgons , unapproachable and unspeakable , longing to seize him : as they trod upon the pale adamant , the shield rang sharp and clear with a loud clanging . Two serpents hung down at their girdles with heads curved forward :

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: λίχμαζον δʼ ἄρα τώ γε · μένει δʼ ἐχάρασσον ὀδόντας
sections.translation: their tongues were flickering , and their teeth gnashing with fury , and their eyes glaring fiercely . And upon the awful heads of the Gorgons great Fear was quaking . And beyond these there were men fighting in warlike harness , some defending their own town and parents

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: λοιγὸν ἀμύνοντες , τοὶ δὲ πραθέειν μεμαῶτες .
sections.translation: from destruction , and others eager to sack it ; many lay dead , but the greater number still strove and fought . The women on well-built towers of bronze were crying shrilly and tearing their cheeks like living beings — the work of famous Hephaestus . 245

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: ἄνδρες δʼ , οἳ πρεσβῆες ἔσαν γῆράς τε μέμαρπεν ,
sections.translation: And the men who were elders and on whom age had laid hold were all together outside the gates , and were holding up their hands to the blessed gods , fearing for their own sons . But these again were engaged in battle : and behind them the dusky Fates , gnashing their white fangs ,

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: δεινωπαὶ βλοσυραί τε δαφοιναί τʼ ἄπληταί τε
sections.translation: lowering , grim , bloody , and unapproachable , struggled for those who were falling , for they all were longing to drink dark blood . So soon as they caught a man overthrown or falling newly wounded , one of them would clasp her great claws about him , and his soul would go down to Hades

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: Τάρταρον ἐς κρυόενθʼ . αἳ δὲ φρένας εὖτʼ ἀρέσαντο
sections.translation: to chilly Tartarus . And when they had satisfied their souls with human blood , they would cast that one behind them , and rush back again into the tumult and the fray . Clotho and Lachesis were over them and Atropos less tall than they , a goddess of no great frame , yet

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: τῶν γε μὲν ἀλλάων προφερής τʼ ἦν πρεσβυτάτη τε .
sections.translation: superior to the others and the eldest of them . And they all made a fierce fight over one poor wretch , glaring evilly at one another with furious eyes and fighting equally with claws and hands . By them stood Darkness of Death , mournful and fearful ,

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: χλωρὴ ἀυσταλέη λιμῷ καταπεπτηυῖα ,
sections.translation: pale , shrivelled , shrunk with hunger , swollen-kneed . Long nails tipped her hands , and she dribbled at the nose , and from her cheeks blood dripped down to the ground . She stood leering hideously , and much dust sodden with tears lay upon her shoulders .

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: δάκρυσι μυδαλέη . παρὰ δʼ εὔπυργος πόλις ἀνδρῶν ·
sections.translation: Next , there was a city of men with goodly towers ; and seven gates of gold , fitted to the lintels , guarded it . The men were making merry with festivities and dances ; some were bringing home a bride to her husband on a well-wheeled car , while the bridalsong swelled high ,

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: τῆλε δʼ ἀπʼ αἰθομένων δαΐδων σέλας εἰλύφαζε
sections.translation: and the glow of blazing torches held by handmaidens rolled in waves afar . And these maidens went before , delighting in the festival ; and after them came frolicsome choirs , the youths singing soft-mouthed to the sound of shrill pipes , while the echo was shivered around them , 280

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: αἳ δʼ ὑπὸ φορμίγγων ἄναγον χορὸν ἱμερόεντα .
sections.translation: and the girls led on the lovely dance to the sound of lyres . Then again on the other side was a rout of young men revelling , with flutes playing ; some frolicking with dance and song , and others were going forward in time with a flute player and laughing . The whole town was filled with mirth and dance and festivity .

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: ἀγλαΐαι τʼ εἶχον . τοὶ δʼ αὖ προπάροιθε πόληος
sections.translation: Others again were mounted on horseback and galloping before the town . And there were ploughmen breaking up the good soil , clothed in tunics girt up . Also there was a wide cornland , and some men were reaping with sharp hooks the stalks

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: βριθόμενα σταχύων , ὡς εἰ Δημήτερος ἀκτήν ·
sections.translation: which bended with the weight of the ears — as if they were reaping Demeter ' s grain : others were binding the sheaves with bands and were spreading the threshing floor . And some held reaping hooks and were gathering the vintage , while others were taking from the reapers into baskets white and black clusters from the long rows of vines

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: βριθομένων φύλλοισι καὶ ἀργυρέῃς ἑλίκεσσιν .
sections.translation: which were heavy with leaves and tendrils of silver . Others again were gathering them into baskets . Beside them was a row of vines

sections.label: Section 297
sections.original: χρύσεος ἦν , κλυτὰ ἔργα περίφρονος Ἡφαίστοιο ,
sections.translation: in gold , the splendid work of cunning Hephaestus :

sections.label: Section 299
sections.original: σειόμενος φύλλοισι καὶ ἀργυρέῃσι κάμαξι ,
sections.translation: it had shivering leaves and stakes of silver

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: βριθόμενος σταφυλῇσι · μελάνθησάν γε μὲν αἵδε .
sections.translation: and was laden with grapes which turned black . [*] And there were men treading out the grapes and others drawing off the liquor . Also there were men boxing and wrestling , and huntsmen chasing swift hares with a leash of sharp-toothed dogs before them , they eager to catch the hares , and the hares eager to escape .

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: πὰρ δʼ αὐτοῖς ἱππῆες ἔχον πόνον , ἀμφὶ δʼ ἀέθλῳ
sections.translation: Next to them were horsemen hard set , and they contended and labored for a prize . The charioteers standing on their well-woven cars , urged on their swift horses with loose rein ; the jointed cars flew along clattering and the naves of the wheels shrieked loudly .

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: οἳ μὲν ἄρʼ ἀίδιον εἶχον πόνον οὐδέ ποτέ σφιν
sections.translation: So they were engaged in an unending toil , and the end with victory came never to them , and the contest was ever unwon . And there was set out for them within the course a great tripod of gold , the splendid work of cunning Hephaestus . And round the rim Ocean was flowing , with a full stream as it seemed ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: πᾶν δὲ συνεῖχε σάκος πολυδαίδαλον , οἳ δὲ κατʼ αὐτὸν
sections.translation: and enclosed all the cunning work of the shield . Over it swans were soaring and calling loudly , and many others were swimming upon the surface of the water ; and near them were shoals of fish . A wonderful thing the great strong shield was to see — even for Zeus the loud-thunderer , by whose will Hephaestus made it

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: ἀρσάμενος παλάμῃσι . τὸ μὲν Διὸς ἄλκιμος υἱὸς
sections.translation: and fitted it with his hands . This shield the valiant son of Zeus wielded masterly , and leaped upon his horse-chariot like the lightning of his father Zeus who holds the aegis , moving lithely . And his charioteer , strong Iolaus , standing upon the car , guided the curved chariot .

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ἀγχίμολον δέ σφʼ ἦλθε θεὰ γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη ,
sections.translation: Then the goddess grey-eyed Athena came near them and spoke winged words , encouraging them : 327 “ Hail , offspring of far-famed Lynceus ! Even now Zeus who reigns over the blessed gods gives you power to slay Cycnus and to strip off his splendid armor .

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: ἐσσυμένως . τότε δή ῥα διόγνητος Ἰόλαος
sections.translation: Then heaven-nurtured Iolaus called terribly to the horses , and at his cry they swiftly whirled the fleet chariot along , raising dust from the plain ; for the goddess bright-eyed Athena put mettle into them by shaking her aegis . And the earth groaned all round them .

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: τοὶ δʼ ἄμυδις προγένοντʼ ἴκελοι πυρὶ ἠὲ θυέλλῃ ,
sections.translation: And they , horse-taming Cycnus and Ares , insatiable in war , came on together like fire or whirlwind . Then their horses neighed shrilly , face to face ; and the echo was shivered all round them . And mighty Heracles spoke first and said to that other :

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: πρηνὴς δʼ ἐν κονίῃσι χαμαὶ πέσεν ἔγχεος ὁρμῇ .
sections.translation: And he fell headlong in the dust upon the ground through the force of my spear-thrust ; then truly he would have been disgraced among the deathless gods , if by my hands he had left behind his bloody spoils .” So said he . But Cycnus the stout spearman cared not to obey him and to pull up the horses that drew his chariot .

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: δὴ τότʼ ἀπʼ εὐπλεκέων δίφρων θόρον αἶψʼ ἐπὶ γαῖαν
sections.translation: Then it was that from their well-woven chariots they both leaped straight to the ground , the son of Zeus and the son of the Lord of War . The charioteers drove near by their horses with beautiful manes , and the wide earth rang with the beat of their hoofs as they rushed along . As when rocks leap forth from the high peak of a great mountain ,

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: πέτραι ἀποθρῴσκωσιν , ἐπʼ ἀλλήλῃς δὲ πέσωσι ,
sections.translation: and fall on one another , and many towering oaks and pines and long-rooted poplars are broken by them as they whirl swiftly down until they reach the plain ; so did they fall on one another with a great shout :

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: πᾶσα δὲ Μυρμιδόνων τε πόλις κλειτή τʼ Ἰαωλκὸς
sections.translation: and all the town of the Myrmidons , and famous Iolcus , and Arne , and Helice , and grassy Anthea echoed loudly at the voice of the two . With an awful cry they closed : and wise Zeus thundered loudly and rained down drops of blood ,

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: σῆμα τιθεὶς πολέμοιο ἑῷ μεγαθαρσέι παιδί .
sections.translation: giving the signal for battle to his dauntless son . As a tusked boar , fearful for a man to see before him in the glens of a mountain , resolves to fight with the huntsmen and whets his white tusks , turning sideways , while foam flows all round his mouth

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: λείβεται , ὄσσε δέ οἱ πυρὶ λαμπετόωντι ἔικτον ,
sections.translation: as he gnashes , and his eyes are like glowing fire , and he bristles the hair on his mane and around his neck —, like him the son of Zeus leaped from his horse chariot . And when the dark-winged whirring grasshopper , perched on a green shoot , begins to sing of summer to men —

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: ἄρχεται , ᾧ τε πόσις καὶ βρῶσις θῆλυς ἐέρση ,
sections.translation: his food and drink is the dainty dew — and all day long from dawn pours forth his voice in the deadliest heat , when Sirius scorches the flesh ( then the beard grows upon the millet which men sow in summer ), when the crude grapes

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: οἷα Διώνυσος δῶκʼ ἀνδράσι χάρμα καὶ ἄχθος ·
sections.translation: which Dionysus gave to men — a joy and a sorrow both — begin to color , in that season they fought and loud rose the clamor . 402 As two lions [*] on either side of a slain deer spring at one another in fury , and there is a fearful snarling and a clashing also of teeth —,

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: οἳ δʼ ὥς τʼ αἰγυπιοὶ γαμψώνυχες , ἀγκυλοχεῖλαι ,
sections.translation: like vultures with crooked talons and hooked beak that fight and scream aloud on a high rock over a mountain goat or fat wild-deer which some active man has shot with an arrow from the string , and himself has wandered away elsewhere ,

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: χώρου ἄιδρις ἐών · οἳ δʼ ὀτραλέως ἐνόησαν ,
sections.translation: not knowing the place ; but they quickly mark it and vehemently do keen battle about it —, like these they two rushed upon one another with a shout . Then Cycnus , eager to kill the son of almighty Zeus , struck upon his shield with a brazen spear ,

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: οὐδʼ ἔρρηξεν χαλκόν · ἔρυτο δὲ δῶρα θεοῖο .
sections.translation: but did not break the bronze ; and the gift of the god saved his foe . But the son of Amphitryon , mighty Heracles , with his long spear struck Cycnus violently in the neck beneath the chin , where it was unguarded between helm and shield . And the deadly spear cut through the two sinews ;

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ἀνδροφόνος μελίη · μέγα γὰρ σθένος ἔμπεσε φωτός .
sections.translation: for the hero ' s full strength lighted on his foe . And Cycnus fell as an oak falls or a lofty pine that is stricken by the lurid thunderbolt of Zeus ; even so he fell , and his armour adorned with bronze clashed about him . Then the stout-hearted son of Zeus let him be ,

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: αὐτὸς δὲ βροτολοιγὸν Ἄρην προσιόντα δοκεύσας ,
sections.translation: and himself watched for the onset of manslaying Ares : fiercely he stared , like a lion who has come upon a body and full eagerly rips the hide with his strong claws and takes away the sweet life with all speed : his dark heart is filled with rage

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: γλαυκιόων δʼ ὄσσοις δεινὸν πλευράς τε καὶ ὤμους
sections.translation: and his eyes glare fiercely , while he tears up the earth with his paws and lashes his flanks and shoulders with his tail so that no one dares to face him and go near to give battle . Even so , the son of Amphitryon , unsated of battle , stood eagerly face to face with Ares ,

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: ἐσσυμένως · ὃ δέ οἱ σχεδὸν ἤλυθεν ἀχνύμενος κῆρ ·
sections.translation: nursing courage in his heart . And Ares drew near him with grief in his heart ; and they both sprang at one another with a cry . As it is when a rock shoots out from a great cliff and whirls down with long bounds , careering eagerly with a roar , and a high crag clashes with it and keeps it there where they strike together ;

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: ὑψηλός · τῷ δὴ συνενείκεται , ἔνθα μιν ἴσχει ·
sections.translation: with no less clamor did deadly Ares , the chariot-borne , rush shouting at Heracles . And he quickly received the attack . 443 But Athena the daughter of aegis-bearing Zeus came to meet Ares , wearing the dark aegis ,

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: δεινὰ δʼ ὑπόδρα ἰδοῦσα ἔπεα πτερόεντα προσηύδα ·
sections.translation: and she looked at him with an angry frown and spoke winged words to him . “ Ares , check your fierce anger and matchless hands ; for it is not ordained that you should kill Heracles , the bold-hearted son of Zeus , and strip off his rich armor . Come , then , cease fighting and do not withstand me .”

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: ὣς ἔφατʼ · ἀλλʼ οὐ πεῖθʼ Ἄρεος μεγαλήτορα θυμόν ,
sections.translation: So said she , but did not move the courageous spirit of Ares . But he uttered a great shout and waving his spears like fire , he rushed headlong at strong Heracles , longing to kill him , and hurled a brazen spear upon the great shield , for he was furiously angry because of his dead son ;

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: ἐν σάκεϊ μεγάλῳ . ἀπὸ δὲ γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη
sections.translation: but bright-eyed Athena reached out from the chariot and turned aside the force of the spear . Then bitter grief seized Ares , and he drew his keen sword and leaped upon bold-hearted Heracles . But as he came on , the son of Amphitryon , unsated of fierce battle ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: μηρὸν γυμνωθέντα σάκευς ὕπο δαιδαλέοιο
sections.translation: shrewdly wounded his thigh where it was exposed under his richly-wrought shield , and tore deep into his flesh with the spear thrust and cast him flat upon the ground . And Panic and Dread quickly drove his smooth-wheeled chariot and horses near him and lifted him from the wide-pathed earth

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: ἐς δίφρον θῆκαν πολυδαίδαλον · αἶψα δʼ ἔπειτα
sections.translation: into his richly-wrought car , and then straight lashed the horses and came to high Olympus . But the son of Alcmena and glorious Iolaus stripped the fine armour off Cycnus ' shoulders and went ,

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: ἵπποις ὠκυπόδεσσιν . ἀτὰρ γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη
sections.translation: and their swift horses carried them straight to the city of Trachis . And bright-eyed Athena went from there to great Olympus and her father ' s house . As for Cycnus , Ceyx buried him and the countless people who lived near the city of the glorious king , in Anthe and the city of the Myrmidons , and famous Iolcus ,

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: Ἄρνην τʼ ἠδʼ Ἑλίκην · πολλὸς δʼ ἠγείρετο λαός ,
sections.translation: and Arne , and Helice : and many people were gathered doing honor to Ceyx , the friend of the blessed gods . But Anaurus , swelled by a rain-storm , blotted out the grave and memorial of Cycnus ; for so Apollo , Leto ' s son , commanded him , because he used to watch for and violently despoil the rich hecatombs

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: ὅστις ἄγοι Πυθοῖδε βίῃ σύλασκε δοκεύων .
sections.translation: that any might bring to Phyto .
